macro_segments.title: Non-Jewish Shechita and the Ambiguous Status of a Slaughtered Non-Kosher Animal

micro_segments.title: Distinguishing between technical problems in shechita versus problems with the shechita procedure itself
Rav Shmuel Yitzchak's ruling on non-Jewish shechita and the question of physical performance
The intermediate status of a slaughtered animal that is food but not yet dead: examination of swallowed objects and tumah
Abaye's ruling that swallowed items in food no longer retain protected status
The application of bestiality laws to demonstrate the middle status between alive and dead

macro_segments.title: Meat Left on Hide After Flaying: The Debate Between Rabbi Yishmael and Rabbi Akiva on Objective Negation

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's position that gathered alal (meat left on skin) constitutes neveilah when a full kezayit is assembled
Rav Huna's qualification that the skin negates the meat only when it came off through flaying
The Gemara's analysis of whether Rabbi Yishmael or Rabbi Akiva is the underlying authority
Rabbi Yishmael's position that subjective intent determines whether meat on hide loses its status as neveilah
Rabbi Akiva's position that the hide objectively negates meat pieces regardless of how they came off

macro_segments.title: Skins as Meat: Parts of Animals Where Skin Has the Status of Flesh for Neveilah and Tumah

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: human skin and the skin of various animals where the covering is like the meat itself
Human skin and the rabbinic decree to prevent potential misuse of human remains for leather products
The debate between Rabbi Yehuda and the Sages regarding whether pig skin differs between wild and domesticated varieties
The definition of a young camel: whether it is measured by age of load-bearing capacity
The definition of a young calf and whether nursing status or age alone determines the criterion
Rabbi Yochanan's ruling against the Mishna by exempting most calf and animal parts from being treated like meat
The Baraita's enumeration of which animal parts have skin that is like meat, and its implications for pigul
Identification and status of various hooves, joints, and body parts of domesticated animals

macro_segments.title: The Skin of Sheratzim (Lizards): Verse-Based Versus Practical Determinations of Skin Status

micro_segments.title: The list of eight sheratzim in the Torah and the question of whether their skin is like their flesh
Rav's approach: using the biblical verse to determine that only the second group of lizards have skin like flesh
The question of why tinshames (a lizard) is omitted from the Mishna despite being in the Torah's verse
Rabbi Yehuda's approach: basing status on physical feel rather than biblical verses
